Real Analysis

Countability, the Real Numbers, and Order

Countability, the Real Numbers, and Order

Countable Sets

Having established what it means for a set to be finite (Lecture 1), we now extend the classification scheme to infinite sets, distinguishing those infinite sets that can still be “listed” from those that cannot.

Finite Set (nnn elements)

A set SSS is said to have nnn elements if there exists a bijection {1,2,…,n}→S\{1,2,\dots,n\} \to S{1,2,…,n}→S.

Denumerable (Countably Infinite)

A set SSS is denumerable if it is infinite and there exists a bijection N→S\mathbb{N} \to SN→S, where N={1,2,3,… }\mathbb{N} = \{1,2,3,\dots\}N={1,2,3,…}.

Countable Set

A set SSS is countable if it is either finite or denumerable.

Two ways to say “countable”

“Countable” is the umbrella term: finite sets and denumerable sets are both countable, but denumerable specifically means infinite and in bijection with N\mathbb{N}N. Compare this to the nnn-element definition from Lecture 1, which bijects with the finite set {1,…,n}\{1,\dots,n\}{1,…,n} rather than with N\mathbb{N}N itself — the two definitions are parallel in form but apply to different regimes.

Equivalent Characterizations

Directly verifying a bijection with N\mathbb{N}N can be inconvenient. The next theorem gives two more flexible tests for countability.

Equivalent Characterizations of Countability

For a set SSS, the following are equivalent:

  • SSS is countable.
  • There exists a surjection N↠S\mathbb{N} \twoheadrightarrow SN↠S.
  • There exists an injection S↪NS \hookrightarrow \mathbb{N}S↪N.

The surjection and injection criteria are often easier to produce than an outright bijection, since we only need to hit every element of SSS at least once (surjection) or embed SSS injectively somewhere inside N\mathbb{N}N (injection) — any such map can be repaired into a genuine bijection.

This immediately gives a subset test analogous to the Set Sizing theorem from Lecture 1:

Subsets of Countable Sets

If AAA is countable and B⊆AB \subseteq AB⊆A, then BBB is countable.

Why this follows quickly

If AAA is countable, fix an injection A↪NA \hookrightarrow \mathbb{N}A↪N. Restricting it to B⊆AB \subseteq AB⊆A still gives an injection B↪NB \hookrightarrow \mathbb{N}B↪N, so by the equivalent characterization above, BBB is countable. This is the “infinite-friendly” analogue of the fact that subsets of finite sets are finite.

Closure Under Intersection and Union

Intersections and Unions of Countable Sets

Let S,TS,TS,T be sets.

  • If at least one of SSS or TTT is countable, then S∩TS \cap TS∩T is countable.
  • If both SSS and TTT are countable, then S∪TS \cup TS∪T is countable.

The intersection statement follows at once from the Subsets Lemma, since S∩TS \cap TS∩T is a subset of whichever of S,TS,TS,T is countable. The union statement is more delicate — it requires actually constructing a bijection — and is the content of the next theorem.

Bijection for the Union of Two Denumerable Sets

If SSS and TTT are denumerable, then S∪TS \cup TS∪T is denumerable.

Proof

The obvious approach — interleaving an enumeration of SSS with one of TTT — fails when SSS and TTT overlap, since elements of S∩TS \cap TS∩T would be listed twice and the resulting map would not be injective. The fix is to enumerate only the new elements TTT contributes.

Since T∖S⊆TT\setminus S \subseteq TT∖S⊆T, the Subsets Lemma guarantees T∖ST \setminus ST∖S is countable. Let f:N→Sf:\mathbb{N}\to Sf:N→S and g:N→T∖Sg:\mathbb{N}\to T\setminus Sg:N→T∖S be bijections, and interleave them:

h(n)={f(k),if n=2k (even)g(k),if n=2k−1 (odd).h(n)= \begin{cases} f(k), & \text{if } n=2k \text{ (even)}\\ g(k), & \text{if } n=2k-1 \text{ (odd)}. \end{cases}h(n)={f(k),g(k),​if n=2k (even)if n=2k−1 (odd).​

Because SSS and T∖ST\setminus ST∖S are disjoint by construction, hhh hits every element of S∪TS \cup TS∪T exactly once, so h:N→S∪Th:\mathbb{N}\to S\cup Th:N→S∪T is a bijection. Explicitly, the sequence begins

h(1)=g(1),h(2)=f(1),h(3)=g(2),h(4)=f(2), …h(1)=g(1),\quad h(2)=f(1),\quad h(3)=g(2),\quad h(4)=f(2),\ \dotsh(1)=g(1),h(2)=f(1),h(3)=g(2),h(4)=f(2), …

The Rationals Are Countable

As a first substantial application, we show that Q\mathbb{Q}Q — despite being dense in R\mathbb{R}R and therefore feeling much “bigger” than N\mathbb{N}N — is in fact countable.

The Set of Rational Numbers is Countable

Q\mathbb{Q}Q is countable.

Proof

It suffices to show Q+={m/n:m,n∈N}\mathbb{Q}^+=\{m/n : m,n\in\mathbb{N}\}Q+={m/n:m,n∈N} is countable: since x↦−xx \mapsto -xx↦−x gives a bijection Q+→Q−\mathbb{Q}^+ \to \mathbb{Q}^-Q+→Q−, the Union Theorem above applied to Q−∪{0}∪Q+\mathbb{Q}^- \cup \{0\} \cup \mathbb{Q}^+Q−∪{0}∪Q+ then finishes the job.

Arrange Q+\mathbb{Q}^+Q+ in a grid indexed by (m,n)∈N2(m,n)\in\mathbb{N}^2(m,n)∈N2:

1/12/13/14/1…
1/22/23/24/2…
1/32/33/34/3…
……………

Traverse along diagonals (e.g., in order of constant m+nm+nm+n), listing each reduced fraction only the first time it appears and skipping duplicates such as 2/2=1/12/2 = 1/12/2=1/1. This produces a bijection N→Q+\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{Q}^+N→Q+, completing the proof.

Countable Unions of Countable Sets

The diagonal-traversal idea used for Q+\mathbb{Q}^+Q+ is not a one-off trick — it generalizes to show that countability is preserved under taking countably many countable pieces at once, strictly extending the two-set Union Theorem above.

Countable Union of Countable Sets

If each AnA_nAn​ is countable, then ⋃n=1∞An\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n⋃n=1∞​An​ is countable.

Proof

For each nnn, fix a surjection f(n):N→Anf^{(n)}:\mathbb{N}\to A_nf(n):N→An​ (possible since each AnA_nAn​ is countable). Arrange the values f(n)(k)f^{(n)}(k)f(n)(k) in a grid with rows indexed by nnn and columns by kkk — exactly as in the Q+\mathbb{Q}^+Q+ construction. Traversing this grid by diagonals and recording each element the first time it appears yields a surjection N→⋃n≥1An\mathbb{N}\to \bigcup_{n\ge1} A_nN→⋃n≥1​An​. By the Equivalent Characterizations theorem, a surjection from N\mathbb{N}N suffices to conclude the union is countable.

The diagonal argument as a unifying technique

Notice the same diagonal-traversal idea powers both the countability of Q\mathbb{Q}Q and this general union theorem — enumerating Q+\mathbb{Q}^+Q+ is really just the special case An={n/1,n/2,n/3,… }A_n = \{n/1, n/2, n/3, \dots\}An​={n/1,n/2,n/3,…}. Recognizing this shared machinery is more useful than memorizing each proof separately.

Algebraic Properties of R\mathbb{R}R

Having studied how many elements various sets have, we now turn to the algebraic and order structure of R\mathbb{R}R itself, starting from its defining axioms.

Field Axioms for R\mathbb{R}R

Two binary operations "+++" (addition) and "⋅\cdot⋅" (multiplication) are defined on R\mathbb{R}R.

  • Addition
    1. Commutativity: a+b=b+aa+b=b+aa+b=b+a
    2. Associativity: (a+b)+c=a+(b+c)(a+b)+c=a+(b+c)(a+b)+c=a+(b+c)
    3. Identity: ∃ 0∈R\exists\,0\in\mathbb{R}∃0∈R such that a+0=aa+0=aa+0=a
    4. Inverses: ∀a∈R ∃ (−a)∈R\forall a\in\mathbb{R}\ \exists\,(-a)\in\mathbb{R}∀a∈R ∃(−a)∈R with a+(−a)=0a+(-a)=0a+(−a)=0
  • Multiplication
    1. Commutativity: a⋅b=b⋅aa\cdot b=b\cdot aa⋅b=b⋅a
    2. Associativity: (a⋅b)⋅c=a⋅(b⋅c)(a\cdot b)\cdot c=a\cdot(b\cdot c)(a⋅b)⋅c=a⋅(b⋅c)
    3. Identity: ∃ 1∈R\exists\,1\in\mathbb{R}∃1∈R such that a⋅1=aa\cdot 1=aa⋅1=a
    4. Inverses: ∀a≠0 ∃ a−1∈R\forall a\neq 0\ \exists\,a^{-1}\in\mathbb{R}∀a=0 ∃a−1∈R with a⋅a−1=1a\cdot a^{-1}=1a⋅a−1=1
  • Distributivity
    1. a⋅(b+c)=a⋅b+a⋅ca\cdot(b+c)=a\cdot b+a\cdot ca⋅(b+c)=a⋅b+a⋅c
Notes
  • Subtraction is addition of inverses: a−b=a+(−b)a-b=a+(-b)a−b=a+(−b).
  • Division is multiplication by inverses: a/b=a⋅b−1a/b=a\cdot b^{-1}a/b=a⋅b−1 (for b≠0b\neq 0b=0).

Basic Consequences

Everything in this subsection is derived purely from the field axioms above — none of it is a new assumption. This is worth dwelling on: facts as “obvious” as a⋅0=0a \cdot 0 = 0a⋅0=0 actually require proof from more primitive axioms.

Additive Cancellation (Special Case)

If z,a∈Rz,a\in\mathbb{R}z,a∈R and z+a=az+a=az+a=a, then z=0z=0z=0.

Proof
z+a=a(z+a)+(−a)=a+(−a)add −az+(a+(−a))=0associativity and inversesz+0=0z=0since z+0=z.\begin{aligned} z+a&=a\\ (z+a)+(-a)&=a+(-a) && \text{add }-a\\ z+(a+(-a))&=0 && \text{associativity and inverses}\\ z+0&=0\\ z&=0 && \text{since }z+0=z. \end{aligned}z+a(z+a)+(−a)z+(a+(−a))z+0z​=a=a+(−a)=0=0=0​​add −aassociativity and inversessince z+0=z.​

The same “add the inverse, then simplify” pattern proves the multiplicative analogue:

Multiplicative Cancellation

If a⋅b=a⋅ca\cdot b=a\cdot ca⋅b=a⋅c and a≠0a\neq 0a=0, then b=cb=cb=c.

a⋅b=a⋅c(a⋅b)⋅a−1=(a⋅c)⋅a−1(a⋅a−1)⋅b=(a⋅a−1)⋅c1⋅b=1⋅cb=c.\begin{aligned} a\cdot b&=a\cdot c\\ (a\cdot b)\cdot a^{-1}&=(a\cdot c)\cdot a^{-1}\\ (a\cdot a^{-1})\cdot b&=(a\cdot a^{-1})\cdot c\\ 1\cdot b&=1\cdot c\\ b&=c. \end{aligned}a⋅b(a⋅b)⋅a−1(a⋅a−1)⋅b1⋅bb​=a⋅c=(a⋅c)⋅a−1=(a⋅a−1)⋅c=1⋅c=c.​
Multiplication by Zero

For all a∈Ra\in\mathbb{R}a∈R, a⋅0=0a\cdot 0=0a⋅0=0.

Proof
a⋅0=a⋅(0+0)=a⋅0+a⋅0distributivitya⋅0=0add −(a⋅0) to both sides.\begin{aligned} a\cdot 0&=a\cdot(0+0)=a\cdot 0+a\cdot 0 && \text{distributivity}\\ a\cdot 0&=0 && \text{add }-(a\cdot 0)\text{ to both sides.} \end{aligned}a⋅0a⋅0​=a⋅(0+0)=a⋅0+a⋅0=0​​distributivityadd −(a⋅0) to both sides.​

With a⋅0=0a \cdot 0 = 0a⋅0=0 established, we can now characterize exactly when a product vanishes — a fact used constantly in algebra and, shortly, in the irrationality proof below.

Zero-Product Property

If a⋅b=0a\cdot b=0a⋅b=0, then a=0a=0a=0 or b=0b=0b=0.

Proof

If a=0a=0a=0 we are done. If a≠0a\neq 0a=0, multiply a⋅b=0a\cdot b=0a⋅b=0 on the left by a−1a^{-1}a−1:

a−1⋅(a⋅b)=a−1⋅0(a−1⋅a)⋅b=01⋅b=0b=0.\begin{aligned} a^{-1}\cdot(a\cdot b)&=a^{-1}\cdot 0\\ (a^{-1}\cdot a)\cdot b&=0\\ 1\cdot b&=0\\ b&=0. \end{aligned}a−1⋅(a⋅b)(a−1⋅a)⋅b1⋅bb​=a−1⋅0=0=0=0.​

Rational Numbers and Irrationality

Rational Numbers

Q={mn | m,n∈Z, n≠0}.\mathbb{Q}=\left\{\frac{m}{n}\ \middle|\ m,n\in\mathbb{Z},\ n\neq 0\right\}.Q={nm​ ​ m,n∈Z, n=0}.

Section 1.3 showed Q\mathbb{Q}Q is countable — that is, “small” in a set-theoretic sense. The next theorem shows Q\mathbb{Q}Q is also incomplete in an algebraic sense: it fails to contain solutions to equations as simple as r2=2r^2 = 2r2=2.

2\sqrt{2}2​ is Irrational

There is no r∈Qr\in\mathbb{Q}r∈Q such that r2=2r^2=2r2=2.

Proof (by contradiction)

Suppose r=m/nr=m/nr=m/n in lowest terms with m,n∈Zm,n\in\mathbb{Z}m,n∈Z, n≠0n\neq 0n=0, and r2=2r^2=2r2=2. Then

m2n2=2 ⇒ m2=2n2.\frac{m^2}{n^2}=2\ \Rightarrow\ m^2=2n^2.n2m2​=2 ⇒ m2=2n2.

Thus m2m^2m2 is even, so mmm is even: write m=2km=2km=2k. Substituting,

2n2=m2=(2k)2=4k2 ⇒ n2=2k2,2n^2=m^2=(2k)^2=4k^2\ \Rightarrow\ n^2=2k^2,2n2=m2=(2k)2=4k2 ⇒ n2=2k2,

so nnn is also even. Then mmm and nnn share the common factor 222, contradicting that m/nm/nm/n was in lowest terms. Therefore 2∉Q\sqrt{2}\notin\mathbb{Q}2​∈/Q.

Existence of irrationals, in context

This theorem is the reason R\mathbb{R}R cannot simply equal Q\mathbb{Q}Q: the reals must contain “gaps” like 2\sqrt{2}2​ that Q\mathbb{Q}Q misses. Combined with the countability of Q\mathbb{Q}Q established earlier, this sets up a natural question for later — just how much “bigger” is R\mathbb{R}R than Q\mathbb{Q}Q? — though answering it requires tools (like the order axioms below and eventually completeness) beyond what we’ve built so far.

Ordering Properties of R\mathbb{R}R

The field axioms alone say nothing about the order "<<<" on R\mathbb{R}R — a field like C\mathbb{C}C satisfies the same axioms but has no natural ordering. Order on R\mathbb{R}R is therefore introduced as a separate structure, built from a distinguished subset of “positive” elements.

Positive Set

Let P⊆RP\subseteq\mathbb{R}P⊆R, P≠∅P\neq\varnothingP=∅, be the set of positive real numbers. It satisfies:

  1. If a,b∈Pa,b\in Pa,b∈P, then a+b∈Pa+b\in Pa+b∈P. (closure under addition)
  2. If a,b∈Pa,b\in Pa,b∈P, then a⋅b∈Pa\cdot b\in Pa⋅b∈P. (closure under multiplication)
  3. Trichotomy: For any a∈Ra\in\mathbb{R}a∈R, exactly one of a∈Pa\in Pa∈P, a=0a=0a=0, or −a∈P-a\in P−a∈P holds.

From this single set PPP, all the familiar order notation and its properties can be defined.

Notation and Ordering
  • If a∈Pa\in Pa∈P, write a>0a>0a>0; if −a∈P-a\in P−a∈P, write a<0a<0a<0.
  • If a∈P∪{0}a\in P\cup\{0\}a∈P∪{0}, write a≥0a\ge 0a≥0; if −a∈P∪{0}-a\in P\cup\{0\}−a∈P∪{0}, write a≤0a\le 0a≤0.
  • Define a>ba>ba>b iff a−b∈Pa-b\in Pa−b∈P; define a≥ba\ge ba≥b iff a−b∈P∪{0}a-b\in P\cup\{0\}a−b∈P∪{0}.
  • For any a,b∈Ra,b\in\mathbb{R}a,b∈R, exactly one of a>ba>ba>b, a=ba=ba=b, or a<ba<ba<b holds (trichotomy).
Order is derived, not assumed piecewise

Every order relation here ultimately reduces to a statement about membership in PPP: a>ba > ba>b means a−b∈Pa - b \in Pa−b∈P, nothing more. This means any order fact you need to prove (e.g. transitivity of >>>, or that a>b⇒a+c>b+ca>b \Rightarrow a+c>b+ca>b⇒a+c>b+c) should be provable directly from the three properties of PPP together with the field axioms from Section 2 — the two structures are designed to interact seamlessly.
